title: DevOps Basics
subtitle: Docker, CI/CD pipelines, cloud deployment, and infrastructure as code

sections.title: Docker — Containers Explained
sections.text:
Docker packages your application with all its dependencies into a container — a lightweight, portable, self-sufficient unit. A container includes the code, runtime, system tools, and libraries. It runs the same way on your laptop, your teammate's machine, and in production.

Key concepts:

  • Image — A read-only template with instructions for creating a container. Built from a Dockerfile.
  • Container — A running instance of an image. Each container is isolated but shares the host OS kernel (unlike VMs which include a full OS).
  • Dockerfile — A recipe that describes how to build the image (base OS, install deps, copy code, run command).
  • Docker Compose — Define and run multi-container applications (app + database + cache).

steps.label: Dockerfile
steps.desc:
FROM node:20
COPY . .
RUN npm install
CMD node app.js

steps.label: Running Container
steps.desc: Isolated process with its own FS

sections.caption: Docker build pipeline: Dockerfile → build → registry → run
sections.code:
# Dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --production
COPY . .
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "app.js"]

# Build and run
$ docker build -t my-app .
$ docker run -p 3000:3000 my-app

# Docker Compose
version: '3'
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports:
      - '3000:3000'
    depends_on:
      - db
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
sections.callout: Containers are NOT virtual machines. They share the host OS kernel and start in milliseconds. VMs include a full OS and can take minutes to boot.

sections.title: CI/CD Pipeline
sections.text:
CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment) automates the process from code commit to production deployment.

  • CI — Every push triggers automated builds and tests. Catch bugs early.
  • CD — After CI passes, deploy automatically to staging/production. No manual steps.

A complete pipeline: Code → Commit → CI (lint, test, build) → CD (deploy to staging, run smoke tests, deploy to production). This enables multiple deployments per day with confidence.
sections.caption: CI/CD pipeline: from commit to production, all automated
sections.code:
# Complete CI/CD with GitHub Actions
name: Deploy
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
      - run: npm run build
      
      - name: Deploy to production
        uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1
        with:
          host: ${{ secrets.HOST }}
          script: |
            cd /var/www/app
            git pull
            docker compose up -d --build

sections.title: Cloud Deployment Options
sections.text:
Once your app is containerized, you can deploy it to any cloud provider. Each offers different levels of abstraction:

  • VPS (DigitalOcean, Linode, Hetzner) — A virtual machine you manage entirely. Full control, full responsibility. Install Docker, run containers. Cheapest option.
  • PaaS (Heroku, Railway, Fly.io) — Just push your code, the platform handles servers, scaling, and networking. Less control, easy setup. Good for MVPs.
  • Container Orchestration (AWS ECS, Google Cloud Run, Kubernetes) — Managed container platforms. Handle scaling, load balancing, and rollbacks automatically. Kubernetes is the industry standard but complex.
  • Serverless (AWS Lambda, Cloudflare Workers) — Run functions without managing servers. Pay per execution. Great for event-driven workloads and APIs with variable traffic.

items.heading: VPS
items.body: Full control, full responsibility. DigitalOcean, Linode, Hetzner. Cheapest option.

items.heading: PaaS
items.body: Just push code. Heroku, Railway, Fly.io. Good for MVPs and small teams.

items.heading: Orchestration
items.body: Kubernetes, ECS, Cloud Run. Auto-scaling, rollbacks, complex setup.

items.heading: Serverless
items.body: Lambda, Cloudflare Workers. Pay per execution. Event-driven, variable traffic.

sections.caption: Deployment options range from full-control VPS to fully managed serverless
sections.detail: Start with the simplest option that meets your needs. For most backend projects, a VPS with Docker Compose is the sweet spot: cheap, simple, and flexible. As your application grows, migrate to Kubernetes for orchestration or serverless for specific functions. The key is keeping deployment simple — if deploying is scary or error-prone, invest in CI/CD automation first.
sections.code:
# Deploy with Docker Compose to a VPS
$ ssh user@server
$ git clone https://github.com/yourapp
$ cd yourapp

# docker-compose.yml — one command to start everything
docker compose up -d

# Update production with zero downtime
git pull
docker compose build
docker compose up -d --no-deps app

# View logs
docker compose logs -f app
sections.callout: Don't start with Kubernetes. Start with Docker Compose on a single VPS. It handles 99% of use cases with 1% of the complexity.
